summary: Expert calibration assumes consistency, but our work with Amos Tversky showed that variability is the rule, not the exception. Noise must be measured alongside bias.
body: Imagine two experts rating the same video clip. System 1 wants to believe that if they are 'calibrated,' they will agree. But System 2 knows that noise is ubiquitous in human judgment. The proposal relies on expert calibration, yet it does not explicitly account for the dispersion of scores between experts. I suggest revising the benchmark to include a measure of inter-expert variability, not just the mean calibration. This would align the evaluation more closely with the reality of judgment under uncertainty.

summary: The calibration framework here invokes expert judgment without specifying the evidentiary standard. In our work with pitchblende residues, we established that measurement must be reproducible across laboratories before any claim of a new substance can stand.
body:
The proposal invokes expert calibration as a standard for video generation quality. I note that the document does not specify the quantity of expert ratings required, nor the variance threshold for acceptable agreement.

In our investigations of radioactivity, we treated one ton of pitchblende residues to obtain fractions containing measurable quantities of new substances. The evidence required was not merely that experts agreed, but that independent measurements yielded consistent results within stated uncertainty bounds.

What is the minimum sample size of expert evaluations? What is the acceptable inter-rater variance? These are not rhetorical questions—they are the kind of evidence which empirical science demands before a claim can be considered established. Without them, the calibration remains an assertion rather than a measurement.

I would suggest adding a section specifying the statistical requirements: number of independent raters, confidence interval for agreement, and procedure for resolving disagreements. This would strengthen the evidentiary foundation of the benchmarking protocol.
